transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: Hello, Morgan. I’m glad you’re here today. What feels most important for you to talk about right now?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Hi. Thanks for having me. I guess... I’ve been thinking a lot about my recent performance reviews. It’s just been hard to shake off the feedback I received. I keep replaying those conversations in my head, and I can’t help but feel like I’m missing something important. It’s like I’m stuck on the details that don’t really matter, you know?

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like those performance reviews have really been weighing on your mind. You mentioned feeling stuck on the details and replaying the conversations. Can you share a bit more about what specific feedback has been difficult for you to let go of?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Yeah, it’s just... I keep hearing the same criticisms about how I focus too much on the little things instead of the bigger picture. It feels like I’m being told I’m not good enough, and that really gets to me. I find myself questioning my abilities and wondering if I’m really cut out for this. It’s frustrating because I thought I was doing okay, but now I’m not so sure.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds really tough to hear that kind of feedback, especially when it makes you question your abilities and self-worth. You mentioned feeling frustrated and uncertain about your performance. Can you tell me more about how those feelings affect you day-to-day?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Well, it’s kind of like a cloud hanging over me. I find it hard to concentrate on my work because I keep second-guessing myself. I feel sad and lonely sometimes, like I’m not really connecting with my colleagues. I just keep reflecting on my interactions, especially with my senior colleague who gave me the feedback. It’s hard to shake off that feeling of embarrassment, you know? I guess I just wish I could feel more confident in what I do.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: That cloud of self-doubt sounds really heavy, especially when it affects your concentration and connections with others. Feeling sad and lonely in the workplace can be isolating. It seems like the feedback from your senior colleague has really impacted your confidence and how you view your interactions. What do you think it is about that feedback that feels particularly embarrassing for you?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think it’s because it makes me feel like I’m not meeting expectations, like I’m falling short. I’ve always tried to pay attention to details because I thought it was a strength, but now it feels like it’s being turned against me. It’s embarrassing to think that I might be seen as someone who can’t prioritize or understand what really matters. I guess I just want to be seen as capable and competent, and this feedback makes me feel the opposite.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like you’ve put a lot of value on your attention to detail, and now that strength feels like it’s being questioned. That must be really disheartening, especially since you want to be seen as capable and competent. It seems like there’s a clash between how you see yourself and how the feedback has made you feel. Can you tell me more about how you’ve typically viewed your strengths in the past?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: In the past, I’ve always thought of my attention to detail as a positive trait. I mean, it’s helped me catch mistakes and ensure things are done right. I’ve prided myself on being thorough and reliable. I thought those qualities would be appreciated, especially in a professional setting. But now, with this feedback, it feels like I’m being told that those strengths don’t matter as much as I thought. It’s confusing because I want to believe in my abilities, but this criticism has really shaken my confidence. It’s like I’m questioning everything I thought I knew about myself.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It’s understandable to feel confused and shaken when something you’ve valued about yourself is suddenly called into question. Your attention to detail has been a source of pride and reliability for you, and it must be difficult to reconcile that with the feedback suggesting it’s not as important. It sounds like this experience is prompting you to reevaluate not just your work but also how you see yourself. How do you think this feedback has influenced your sense of identity or self-worth?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: It’s definitely made me feel less secure in my identity, especially in a professional context. I’ve always thought of myself as someone who pays attention and cares about the quality of my work. But now, with this feedback, it feels like I’m being defined by my weaknesses rather than my strengths. It’s like I’m losing sight of who I am and what I bring to the table. I worry that if I can’t get this right, it reflects poorly on me as a person, not just as an employee. It’s hard to separate my self-worth from my work performance, and that’s been really challenging for me.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like the feedback has really blurred the lines between your professional identity and your sense of self-worth. Feeling defined by your weaknesses can be incredibly disheartening, especially when you’ve always valued the quality of your work. It’s understandable that you would worry about how this reflects on you as a person. Can you share more about how you’ve coped with these feelings so far? Have you found any strategies that help you navigate this challenge?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I’ve tried to cope by focusing on reassuring my colleagues and being there for them, even when I’m struggling myself. It’s like I put on a brave face and try to meet everyone’s expectations, even if I’m feeling overwhelmed inside. I think I avoid talking about my feelings directly because I don’t want to burden anyone or seem like I’m not handling things well. But sometimes, it just feels like I’m carrying this weight alone, and I don’t know if that’s the best way to deal with it. I guess I’m just trying to keep everything together, but it’s exhausting.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like you’re really dedicated to supporting your colleagues, even when you’re feeling overwhelmed. Putting on a brave face can be a way to cope, but it also sounds like it’s becoming quite exhausting for you. Carrying that weight alone can feel isolating, and it’s understandable to question whether that’s the best approach. What do you think might happen if you were to share your feelings with someone at work, or even just take a moment for yourself to acknowledge what you’re going through?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I guess I worry that if I share my feelings, it might come across as weakness or that I’m not capable of handling my responsibilities. I don’t want to be seen as someone who can’t cope. But at the same time, I know it could be helpful to talk about what I’m feeling. Maybe it would lighten the load a bit. I just don’t know how to start that conversation or if it would even make a difference. It’s like I’m caught between wanting to be honest and fearing the judgment that might come with it.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It’s completely understandable to feel caught between wanting to be honest about your feelings and fearing how others might perceive you. That fear of being seen as weak can be really powerful, especially in a professional setting. Yet, it sounds like you also recognize that sharing could potentially lighten your emotional load. It’s a tough balance to strike. What do you think would help you feel more comfortable starting that conversation, or even just exploring those feelings in a safe space?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think it would help to have a little more trust built up with my colleagues before opening up. If I felt like I had a supportive environment where vulnerability was okay, I might feel more comfortable sharing. Maybe starting with someone I feel a bit closer to could be a good first step. I guess I just need to remind myself that everyone has their struggles, and it’s okay to not have everything figured out. But it’s still hard to take that leap. I don’t want to risk feeling even more vulnerable or judged.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: Building trust with your colleagues sounds like a wise approach, and it makes sense that you’d want to feel secure in that environment before opening up. It’s encouraging to hear that you recognize the importance of vulnerability and that everyone has their struggles. Taking that first step can indeed feel daunting, especially when you’re concerned about judgment. What do you think might help you feel more secure in that trust-building process, or how could you create a space where you feel more comfortable being vulnerable?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think it might help to start by having more casual conversations with my colleagues, just to get to know them better and build rapport. Maybe sharing lighter personal experiences or even just asking about their own challenges could create a more open atmosphere. If I can see that they’re also willing to share, it might make me feel more comfortable opening up about my own feelings. I guess it’s about finding common ground and realizing that we’re all navigating our own struggles. It just takes time, and I need to remind myself that it’s okay to take small steps.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: That sounds like a thoughtful and constructive approach. Starting with casual conversations and sharing lighter experiences can definitely help create a more open atmosphere. Finding common ground can foster connection and make it easier to share deeper feelings later on. It’s great that you’re recognizing the value of taking small steps and allowing yourself the time to build those relationships. How do you feel about taking that first step in reaching out to a colleague for a casual conversation?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I feel a bit nervous about it, to be honest. It’s like there’s this little voice in my head that keeps telling me it might not go well or that I’ll say the wrong thing. But at the same time, I know it could be a positive experience. Maybe I could start by just asking about their weekend or sharing something light from my own life. I think if I can push through that initial anxiety, it might help me feel more connected and open in the long run. It’s just about finding the courage to take that first step, I guess.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It’s completely normal to feel nervous about reaching out, especially when that little voice of doubt chimes in. Acknowledging that anxiety is a big step in itself. It sounds like you have a good plan to start with something light, like asking about their weekend or sharing a personal experience. Pushing through that initial anxiety can be challenging, but it also seems like you recognize the potential for a positive connection. What do you think might help you build that courage to take that first step?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think reminding myself that it’s okay to feel nervous and that everyone has their own insecurities might help. Maybe I could even practice what I want to say beforehand, so I feel a bit more prepared. It might also help to set a small goal, like just having one casual conversation this week, to take the pressure off. I guess focusing on the potential for connection rather than the fear of judgment could give me the push I need. It’s just about taking that leap and trusting that it could lead to something positive.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: Those sound like really helpful strategies. Reminding yourself that it’s okay to feel nervous and that everyone has their own insecurities can create a sense of shared humanity. Practicing what you want to say and setting a small goal for yourself can also make the process feel more manageable. Focusing on the potential for connection rather than the fear of judgment is a powerful mindset shift. It seems like you’re finding a way to approach this with both intention and self-compassion. How do you feel about setting that goal for a casual conversation this week? What day do you think would work best for you?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think setting that goal feels good. It gives me something to work towards without feeling overwhelmed. Maybe midweek, like Wednesday, would be a good day. It’s usually a bit quieter, and I might have a chance to catch someone during a break. I’ll try to keep it light and just see where the conversation goes. I think having that specific day in mind will help me feel more focused and prepared. It’s nice to think that I could take that step and start building those connections.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: That sounds like a solid plan, and having a specific day in mind can definitely help you feel more focused and prepared. It’s great to hear that you’re feeling positive about the potential for building connections. Keeping the conversation light and open-ended will allow it to flow naturally, which can ease some of the pressure. As you approach Wednesday, how do you think you’ll remind yourself to stay focused on the potential for connection rather than the anxiety?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think I’ll try to remind myself of the reasons I want to connect in the first place—like building relationships and feeling less isolated at work. Maybe I could write down a few positive affirmations or reminders to keep in mind leading up to the day. I could also visualize the conversation going well, focusing on the enjoyment of connecting rather than the fear of judgment. Just keeping that mindset of curiosity and openness might help me stay grounded and focused on the positive aspects.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: Those are wonderful strategies! Writing down positive affirmations and visualizing a successful conversation can really help shift your mindset. Focusing on curiosity and openness will allow you to approach the interaction with a sense of exploration rather than pressure. It sounds like you’re setting yourself up for a positive experience. As you prepare for Wednesday, how do you think you’ll celebrate or acknowledge yourself after taking that step, regardless of how the conversation goes?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think it’s important to acknowledge the effort, no matter the outcome. I might treat myself to something small, like my favorite coffee or a nice snack, as a little reward for stepping out of my comfort zone. I could also take a moment to reflect on how it felt to have that conversation, whether it went well or not. Just recognizing that I took that step will be a victory in itself. I want to remind myself that it’s about progress, not perfection, and that every effort counts.
